valproate|lamotrigine.mechanism: Valproate inhibits UGT-mediated glucuronidation of lamotrigine (primarily UGT2B7 and UGT1A4), reducing lamotrigine clearance.
valproate|lamotrigine.clinical_consequence: Increased lamotrigine exposure with higher risk of rash including Stevens–Johnson syndrome and CNS toxicity.

digoxin|verapamil.mechanism: Verapamil inhibits intestinal and renal P-glycoprotein (ABCB1), reducing efflux and clearance of digoxin.
digoxin|verapamil.clinical_consequence: Marked increase in digoxin levels and risk of digoxin toxicity (arrhythmias, GI symptoms, visual disturbances).

amiodarone|digoxin.mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein-mediated efflux of digoxin and may reduce its renal clearance.
amiodarone|digoxin.clinical_consequence: Increased digoxin concentration leading to bradyarrhythmias and other digoxin toxicity manifestations.

digoxin|quinidine.mechanism: Quinidine potently inhibits P-glycoprotein and renal tubular secretion of digoxin.
digoxin|quinidine.clinical_consequence: Substantial increase in digoxin concentration with high risk of life-threatening digoxin toxicity.

simvastatin|gemfibrozil.mechanism: Gemfibrozil and its acyl-glucuronide metabolite inhibit OATP1B1 uptake and CYP2C8/UGT-mediated metabolism of some statins including simvastatin.
simvastatin|gemfibrozil.clinical_consequence: Marked increase in active simvastatin exposure and greatly increased risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.

atorvastatin|gemfibrozil.mechanism: Gemfibrozil inhibits hepatic uptake and glucuronidation of atorvastatin via OATP1B1 and UGT pathways.
atorvastatin|gemfibrozil.clinical_consequence: Increased atorvastatin concentrations and increased risk of statin-associated muscle symptoms and rhabdomyolysis.

simvastatin|clarithromycin.mechanism: Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of simvastatin and its active acid form.
simvastatin|clarithromycin.clinical_consequence: Marked increase in simvastatin exposure with high risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.

simvastatin|itraconazole.mechanism: Itraconazole is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor that markedly reduces the metabolism of simvastatin.
simvastatin|itraconazole.clinical_consequence: Marked elevation of simvastatin levels and high risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.

atorvastatin|clarithromycin.mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of atorvastatin.
atorvastatin|clarithromycin.clinical_consequence: Increased atorvastatin exposure and increased risk of statin-associated muscle toxicity.

atorvastatin|itraconazole.mechanism: Itraconazole strongly inhibits CYP3A4 metabolism of atorvastatin.
atorvastatin|itraconazole.clinical_consequence: Marked rise in atorvastatin levels with elevated risk of myopathy.

tacrolimus|fluconazole.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4/3A5-mediated metabolism of tacrolimus.
tacrolimus|fluconazole.clinical_consequence: Large increases in tacrolimus trough levels, leading to nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, and other calcineurin-inhibitor toxicities.

tacrolimus|itraconazole.mechanism: Itraconazole strongly inhibits CYP3A4/3A5-mediated tacrolimus clearance.
tacrolimus|itraconazole.clinical_consequence: Very large tacrolimus exposure increases, high risk of nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity without dose reduction and close TDM.

cyclosporine|erythromycin.mechanism: Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of cyclosporine.
cyclosporine|erythromycin.clinical_consequence: Increased cyclosporine levels with heightened risk of nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity.

cyclosporine|diltiazem.mechanism: Diltiazem inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp processes that clear cyclosporine.
cyclosporine|diltiazem.clinical_consequence: Increased cyclosporine concentrations and increased risk of nephrotoxicity.

warfarin|amiodarone.mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9, CYP3A4 and CYP1A2, reducing clearance of S- and R-warfarin.
warfarin|amiodarone.clinical_consequence: Increased warfarin effect, elevated INR and substantially increased bleeding risk.

warfarin|fluconazole.mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9-mediated metabolism of S-warfarin and CYP3A4 to a lesser degree.
warfarin|fluconazole.clinical_consequence: Marked INR elevation and bleeding risk when combined without warfarin dose reduction and close monitoring.

warfarin|metronidazole.mechanism: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9-mediated metabolism of S-warfarin.
warfarin|metronidazole.clinical_consequence: INR elevation and increased bleeding risk.

warfarin|trimethoprim_sulfamethoxazole.mechanism: TMP-SMX inhibits CYP2C9 metabolism of S-warfarin and may displace warfarin from plasma proteins.
warfarin|trimethoprim_sulfamethoxazole.clinical_consequence: Substantial INR rises and bleeding risk, often requiring significant warfarin dose reduction.

warfarin|rifampin.mechanism: Rifampin strongly induces CYP2C9, CYP3A4 and other enzymes, increasing warfarin clearance.
warfarin|rifampin.clinical_consequence: Marked reduction of INR and risk of thrombosis unless warfarin dose is substantially increased.

theophylline|ciprofloxacin.clinical_consequence: Increased theophylline levels, raising risk of nausea, seizures, and arrhythmias.

theophylline|fluvoxamine.mechanism: Fluvoxamine strongly inhibits CYP1A2, markedly reducing theophylline clearance.
theophylline|fluvoxamine.clinical_consequence: Major increases in theophylline levels with high risk of serious toxicity.

methotrexate_high_dose|nsaid.mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow via prostaglandin inhibition and compete with methotrexate for renal tubular secretion.
methotrexate_high_dose|nsaid.clinical_consequence: Delayed high-dose methotrexate clearance with increased risk of severe myelosuppression, mucositis, and nephrotoxicity.

methotrexate|probenecid.mechanism: Probenecid inhibits organic anion transporters (OAT) in the renal tubule, reducing methotrexate secretion.
methotrexate|probenecid.clinical_consequence: Prolonged methotrexate exposure with increased toxicity risk.

carbamazepine|oral_contraceptive_combine.mechanism: Carbamazepine is a strong inducer of CYP3A4 and UGTs, increasing the metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and many progestins.
carbamazepine|oral_contraceptive_combine.clinical_consequence: Reduced contraceptive hormone exposure and increased risk of unintended pregnancy and breakthrough bleeding.

phenytoin|oral_contraceptive_combined.mechanism: Phenytoin is a strong hepatic enzyme inducer (CYP3A4 and UGTs), enhancing metabolism of contraceptive steroids.
phenytoin|oral_contraceptive_combined.clinical_consequence: Decreased contraceptive effectiveness and increased risk of unintended pregnancy.

rifampin|oral_contraceptive_combined.mechanism: Rifampin strongly induces CYP3A4 and other enzymes, markedly increasing metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and progestins.
rifampin|oral_contraceptive_combined.clinical_consequence: Substantial loss of contraceptive protection and high risk of unintended pregnancy.

ritonavir|prednisolone.mechanism: Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A inhibitor, reducing clearance of prednisolone and other CYP3A-metabolized corticosteroids.
ritonavir|prednisolone.clinical_consequence: Increased systemic steroid exposure leading to iatrogenic Cushing’s syndrome and adrenal suppression.

ritonavir|fluticasone.mechanism: Ritonavir strongly inhibits CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of inhaled or intranasal fluticasone.
ritonavir|fluticasone.clinical_consequence: Marked systemic corticosteroid accumulation with risk of Cushing’s syndrome and adrenal suppression even from inhaled doses.

clopidogrel|omeprazole.mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, the key enzyme responsible for converting clopidogrel to its active thiol metabolite.
clopidogrel|omeprazole.clinical_consequence: Reduced generation of active clopidogrel metabolite and potentially reduced antiplatelet efficacy with increased risk of thrombotic events in some patients.

metoprolol|paroxetine.mechanism: Paroxetine is a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor, reducing CYP2D6-mediated clearance of metoprolol.
metoprolol|paroxetine.clinical_consequence: Increased metoprolol exposure with greater risk of bradycardia, hypotension, and fatigue.

tamoxifen|paroxetine.mechanism: Paroxetine strongly inhibits CYP2D6, the key enzyme that converts tamoxifen into its active metabolite endoxifen.
tamoxifen|paroxetine.clinical_consequence: Reduced endoxifen levels and potentially reduced efficacy of tamoxifen in estrogen receptor–positive breast cancer.

dabigatran|amiodarone.mechanism: Dabigatran etexilate is a P-gp substrate; amiodarone inhibits P-gp and reduces dabigatran efflux.
dabigatran|amiodarone.clinical_consequence: Elevated dabigatran exposure and increased bleeding risk, particularly in renal impairment.

dabigatran|verapamil.mechanism: Verapamil inhibits P-gp in the gut, increasing dabigatran etexilate absorption.
dabigatran|verapamil.clinical_consequence: Higher dabigatran plasma levels with increased bleeding risk.

rivaroxaban|ketoconazole.mechanism: Rivaroxaban is a substrate of CYP3A4 and P-gp/BCRP; ketoconazole potently inhibits both CYP3A4 and P-gp.
rivaroxaban|ketoconazole.clinical_consequence: Substantial increase in rivaroxaban exposure and bleeding risk.

rivaroxaban|clarithromycin.mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-gp, reducing rivaroxaban clearance.
rivaroxaban|clarithromycin.clinical_consequence: Increased rivaroxaban exposure and bleeding risk.

lamotrigine.notes: Mainly cleared by hepatic glucuronidation via UGT1A4, with UGT2B7 also contributing.

valproate.notes: Undergoes extensive glucuronidation and can inhibit UGT2B7 and other UGT isoforms.

digoxin.notes: Prototype P-gp substrate with negligible CYP metabolism; narrow therapeutic index.

verapamil.notes: Calcium channel blocker; both a P-gp substrate and inhibitor.

amiodarone.notes: Antiarrhythmic drug that inhibits P-gp and multiple CYPs.

quinidine.notes: Potent P-gp inhibitor; classic cause of increased digoxin levels.

cyclosporine.notes: Calcineurin inhibitor; both a P-gp substrate and inhibitor; also inhibits hepatic OATP1B1.

tacrolimus.notes: Calcineurin inhibitor; sensitive CYP3A4/3A5 and P-gp substrate.

simvastatin.notes: Lipid-lowering statin; high first-pass hepatic uptake via OATP1B1; glucuronidation forms active and inactive metabolites.

atorvastatin.notes: Hepatic uptake via OATP1B1 and metabolism by CYP3A4 and UGTs.

pravastatin.notes: Hydrophilic statin; primarily a substrate of OATP1B1 with minimal CYP metabolism.

rosuvastatin.notes: Hydrophilic statin; disposition heavily influenced by OATP and BCRP transport.

gemfibrozil.notes: Fibrate; its acyl-glucuronide metabolite inhibits OATP1B1 and CYP2C8, which underlies key statin interactions.

dabigatran.notes: Dabigatran etexilate is a P-gp substrate; absorption and exposure are sensitive to P-gp modulators, particularly in renal impairment.

rivaroxaban.notes: Factor Xa inhibitor; cleared by combined CYP3A4/5 and transport via P-gp/BCRP.

methotrexate.notes: Renally cleared anticancer/DMARD drug; elimination depends on glomerular filtration and active tubular secretion via OAT1/3 and BCRP.

probenecid.notes: Uricosuric; inhibits renal organic anion transporters, thereby reducing tubular secretion of methotrexate and many other anionic drugs.

rifampin.notes: Powerful inducer of many enzymes and transporters; acutely inhibits and chronically induces OATP1B1, affecting statin disposition.

clopidogrel.notes: Prodrug activated by CYP2C19; minor P-gp substrate role; interaction with omeprazole is mainly CYP2C19-mediated rather than transporter-based.

metoprolol.notes: Primarily a CYP2D6 substrate; no major clinically relevant transporter interactions recognized.

paroxetine.notes: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; clinically important as a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor, not via transporters.

tamoxifen.notes: Prodrug converted to endoxifen mainly via CYP2D6; transporter roles are less clinically central than CYP interactions.
